title: Listening Port and Service Baseline Deviation on BES Servers

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing to partial: UC provides meaningful monitoring evidence for CIP-007-6 R1 Part 1.2.

compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.13.28' running on index=os sourcetype=netstat or scripted listening ports, bes_port_baseline.csv, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

app: Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742)
spl:
index=os (sourcetype=netstat OR sourcetype=listening_ports) earliest=-24h
| lookup bes_cyber_asset_inventory.csv host OUTPUT bes_system
| where isnotnull(bes_system)
| stats latest(port) as port latest(proto) as proto by host, port
| lookup bes_port_baseline.csv host, port OUTPUT approved
| where isnull(approved)
| table host, port, proto, bes_system
description: We compare live listening ports from host telemetry to an approved baseline so unauthorized services on BES Cyber Assets are detected quickly.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NERC CIP compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes os and sourcetypes listening_ports, netstat, netstat`. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Collect listening port data via UF scripted input. (2) Build baseline from golden images. (3) Alert on new ports for seven days. (4) Feed changes into CIP-010 change tickets. Indexes required: `index=os`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=listening_ports`, `sourcetype=netstat`, `sourcetype=netstat``; Lookups: `bes_cyber_asset_inventory`, `bes_cyber_asset_inventory.csv`, `bes_port_baseline`, `bes_port_baseline.csv`.
visualization: Table (new ports), Bar chart (by host), Single value (distinct deviations)

grandmaExplanation: We watch for we compare live listening ports from host telemetry to an approved baseline so unauthorized services on bes cyber assets are detected quickly. This matters because it catches problems that could lead to audit findings or compliance gaps.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=os` `sourcetype=netstat` or scripted listening ports, `bes_port_baseline.csv`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Collect listening port data via UF scripted input. (2) Build baseline from golden images. (3) Alert on new ports for seven days. (4) Feed changes into CIP-010 change tickets.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=os (sourcetype=netstat OR sourcetype=listening_ports) earliest=-24h
| lookup bes_cyber_asset_inventory.csv host OUTPUT bes_system
| where isnotnull(bes_system)
| stats latest(port) as port latest(proto) as proto by host, port
| lookup bes_port_baseline.csv host, port OUTPUT approved
| where isnull(approved)
| table host, port, proto, bes_system
```

Understanding this SPL

**Listening Port and Service Baseline Deviation on BES Servers (CIP-007-6 R1 Part 1.2)** — We compare live listening ports from host telemetry to an approved baseline so unauthorized services on BES Cyber Assets are detected quickly.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=os` `sourcetype=netstat` or scripted listening ports, `bes_port_baseline.csv`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: os; **sourcetype**: netstat, listening_ports. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=os, sourcetype=netstat, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• Filters the current rows with `where isnotnull(bes_system)` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `stats` aggregates events by **host, port**, producing one row per unique combination for trend analysis and threshold evaluation.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• Filters the current rows with `where isnull(approved)` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

Output fields: bes_system, host, port, proto.

Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (new ports), Bar chart (by host), Single value (distinct deviations)

Scripted input (generic example)
This use case relies on a scripted input. In the app's local/inputs.conf add a stanza such as:

```ini
[script://$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/YourApp/bin/collect.sh]
interval = 300
sourcetype = your_sourcetype
index = main
disabled = 0
```

The script should print one event per line (e.g. key=value). Example minimal script (bash):

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Output metrics or events, one per line
echo "metric=value timestamp=$(date +%s)"
```

For full details (paths, scheduling, permissions), see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md
